question: List and briefly define the two main categories of bus arbitration methods.
expectedPoints: Centralized Arbitration: A single hardware device, known as the bus arbiter or controller, is responsible for allocating bus access to requesting devices.
Distributed Arbitration: There is no central arbiter; each device on the bus contains logic to determine its own priority and claim bus access according to a predefined rule set.

question: Explain the daisy-chain arbitration method, including its primary advantage and significant disadvantage.
expectedPoints: Description: A centralized method where the bus grant signal is daisy-chained serially through devices. The arbiter sends a grant signal to the highest-priority device; if that device isn't requesting, it passes the grant to the next device in the chain.
Advantage: It is simple and requires few control lines (e.g., Bus Request, Bus Grant).

Disadvantage: It has a fixed, physical priority. A device's priority is determined by its proximity to the arbiter, and a malfunctioning device can break the chain, blocking access for all lower-priority devices.

question: Compare and contrast the operation and characteristics of the independent request (parallel priority) and distributed arbitration using collision detection (CSMA/CD) methods.
expectedPoints: Independent Request: A centralized method where each device has a dedicated request line to the arbiter and a dedicated grant line from it. The arbiter uses an internal priority algorithm (e.g., fixed priority, round-robin) to choose which device gets the bus. It offers high speed and flexible priority but requires many control lines (2n for n devices).

CSMA/CD: A distributed method used in Ethernet. Devices listen to the bus (carrier sense) and transmit only if it is idle. If two devices transmit simultaneously (collision), they detect it, stop, and wait a random time before retrying. It is efficient under low load but performance degrades under high load due to collisions.

Key Contrast: Independent request uses a central authority and dedicated wiring, while CSMA/CD is decentralized and relies on probabilistic contention. The former is deterministic and predictable, while the latter is stochastic and best-effort.
